result.markdown:
--- title: "Statistical inference for 1D categorical data" subtitle: "
36-315: Statistical Graphics and Visualization, Summer 2026" footer: "[36-315-summer26.netlify.app](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app)" format: revealjs: embed-resources: true height: 900 width: 1600 theme: theme.scss smaller: true slide-number: c/t linestretch: 1.4 code-line-numbers: false html-math-method: method: mathjax url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js" --- ## What does a bar chart show? ::: columns ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(tidyverse) theme_set(theme_light()) penguins |> ggplot(aes(y = species)) + geom_bar() ``` ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## What does a bar chart show? **Marginal distribution**: probability that a categorical variable $X$ (e.g., `species`) takes each particular category value $x$ (`Adelie`, `Chinstrap`, `Gentoo`) * Frequency bar charts give info about sample size ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} table(penguins$species) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo 152 68 124 ``` ::: ::: * Now, we create a proportion/percent bar chart to display the individual probabilities * This shows the probability mass function (PMF) for discrete variables * e.g., $P($`species` $=$ `Adelie`$)$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} prop.table(table(penguins$species)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo 0.4418605 0.1976744 0.3604651 ``` ::: ::: ## Proportion bar charts ::: columns ::: {.column} * Compute proportions "by hand" with `count()` and `mutate()` * Use the pipe operator `|>` to perform multiple operations * Use `geom_col()`, since we want the heights of the bars to represent values in the data ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins |> count(species) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` species n prop 1 Adelie 152 0.4418605 2 Chinstrap 68 0.1976744 3 Gentoo 124 0.3604651 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins |> count(species) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |> ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) + geom_col() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Statistical inference for proportions * Estimate $P($`species` = $C_j$ ) with $\hat p_j$ for each category $C_j$ ( $\hat p_\texttt{Adelie}$, $\hat p_\texttt{Chinstrap}$, $\hat p_\texttt{Gentoo}$ ) ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} prop.table(table(penguins$species)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Adelie Chinstrap Gentoo 0.4418605 0.1976744 0.3604651 ``` ::: ::: * Quantify uncertainty for $\displaystyle \hat p_j = \frac{n_j}{n}$ with the standard error $$\textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_j(1 - \hat{p}_j)}{n}}$$ * Compute $\alpha$-level **confidence interval** $\hat{p}_j \pm z_{1 - \alpha / 2} \cdot \textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j)$ * Good rule-of-thumb: construct 95% confidence interval using $\hat{p}_j \pm 2 \cdot \textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j)$ :::aside The CI is based on a normal approximation justfied by the Central Limit Theorem. (Note: the CI could include values outside of $[0,1]$...)
To get CI for counts, just multiply $\hat p$ by $n$. There are [more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binomial_proportion_confidence_interval) confidence intervals for proportions. ::: ## Adding confidence intervals to bar chart ::: columns ::: {.column}
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins |> count(species) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n), se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)), lower = prop - 2 * se, upper = prop + 2 * se) |> ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) + geom_col() + geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), color = "blue", width = 0.2, linewidth = 1) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Ordering factors in a bar chart ::: columns ::: {.column} Order the bars by proportion ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins |> count(species) |> mutate(prop = n / sum(n), se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)), lower = prop - 2 * se, upper = prop + 2 * se, species = fct_reorder(species, prop)) |> ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) + geom_col() + geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), color = "blue", width = 0.2, linewidth = 1) ``` ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Don't do this... (Look closely...)
![](https://retractionwatch.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/3802603.fig_.009-768x512.png){height=700}
## Hypothesis testing in general * Define the null and alternative hypotheses . . . * Construct the test statistic . . . * Compute the $p$-value * The $p$-value is the probability of observing a test statistic at least as extreme as the observed statistic, under the assumption that null is true * Is test statistic "unusual" compared to what we would expect under the null? . . . * Decide whether to reject the null hypothesis * Compare $p$-value to the target error rate (or significance level) $\alpha$ * Typically choose $\alpha = 0.05$ ([the origins of 0.05](https://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/528Readings/CowlesDavis1982.pdf)) * In other words, if we reject the null hypothesis at $\alpha = 0.05$, then, assuming $H_0$ is true, there is a 5% chance it is a false positive (also known as Type 1 error) ## Chi-squared test for 1D categorical data ::: columns ::: {.column} * Null hypothesis: $H_0$: $p_1 = p_2 = \cdots = p_K$ * Test statistic: $\displaystyle \chi^2 = \sum_{j=1}^K \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$, where * $O_j$: observed counts in category $j$ * $E_j$ : expected counts under $H_0$
(each category is equally likely to occur with probability $n/K = p_1 = p_2 = \cdots = p_K$) ::: ::: {.column}
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} chisq.test(table(penguins$species)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Chi-squared test for given probabilities data: table(penguins$species) X-squared = 31.907, df = 2, p-value = 1.179e-07 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Graphics versus statistical inference ::: columns ::: {.column width="57%"} Reminder: **Anscombe’s Quartet** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="43%"}
* Statistical inference is the same,
but the graphics are very different * The opposite can be true! * Graphics can be the same,
but statistical inference is very different ::: ::: ## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672} ::: ::: ## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672} ::: ::: ## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672} ::: ::: ## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-18-1.png){fig-align='center' width=672} ::: ::: ## Test results for different sample sizes ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ## How do we combine graphics with inference? * Simply add $p$-values (or other info) to graph via text * Add confidence intervals to the graph * Need to remember what each CI is for * The CIs on previous slides are for each $\hat{p}_j$ marginally, **NOT** jointly * Have to be careful with **multiple testing** ## Confidence intervals visually capture uncertainty ::: columns ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-21-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## (Rough) Rules-of-thumb for comparing CIs on bar charts Comparing overlap of two CIs is **NOT** exactly the same as directly testing for a significant difference * Really we want CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ ), not CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ ) * CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ ) not overlapping implies $0 \notin$ CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ ) * CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ ) overlapping **DOES NOT** imply $0 \in$ CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ ) . . . Roughly speaking: * If CIs don't overlap: significant difference * If CIs overlap a little: ambiguous * If CIs overlap a lot: no significant difference . . . But if we're comparing more than two CIs simultaneously, we need to account for **multiple testing** * When you look for all non-overlapping CIs: implicitly making $\displaystyle \binom{K}{2}$ pairwise tests in your head ## Corrections for multiple testing * In those bar plots, when we determine whether CIs overlap, we make 3 comparisons: 1. A vs B 2. A vs C 3. B vs C . . . * **This is a multiple testing (or multiple comparison) problem** . . . * In short: we will make Type 1 errors (chance of false rejection) more than 5% of the time! * Type 1 error: reject $H_0$ when $H_0$ is true * e.g., CIs don't overlap but actually $H_0: p_A = p_B$ is true * If we are only interested in comparing A vs B and nothing else, then just construct 95% CI for A vs B and control error rate at 5% * However, if we construct several CIs, where A vs B is just one comparison we make, our Type 1 error rate is > 5%! ## Corrections for multiple testing Basic idea: * Corrections for multiple testing inflate $p$-values (i.e., make them bigger) * Equivalently, they inflate CIs (i.e., make them wider) * Goal: control Type 1 error rate $\leq 5\%$ . . . **Bonferroni correction**, which inflates $p$-values the most but is simple and easy to implement . . . * We usually reject null hypothesis when $p$-value $\leq 0.05$ * **Bonferroni**: if making $K$ comparisons, reject only if $p$-value $\leq 0.05/K$ * For CIs: instead of plotting 95% CIs, we plot $(1 - 0.05/K)%$% CIs * e.g., when $K = 3$, plot 98.33% CIs ## Impact of Bonferroni correction on confidence intervals ::: columns ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-22-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](lecture03_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){fig-align='center' width=960} ::: ::: ::: ::: ## Takeaways * Graphics for 1D categorical data (e.g., bar charts) show the empirical distribution
of the categorical variable ( $\hat{p}_1, \dots, \hat{p}_K$ ) * Chi-squared test is a common test for 1D categorical data, testing $H_0 : p_1 = \cdots = p_K$ * However, from this (global) test alone, we can’t tell which probabilities differ * We can compute individual confidence intervals for each $\hat{p}_1, \cdots, \hat{p}_K$ * Allows for easy visualization * But can be complicated, especially with respect to multiple testing * Graphs with the same trends can display very different statistical significance (largely due to sample size)

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
